title: Voice

infobox.value: consists of [sound](/wiki/Sound "Sound") [made](/wiki/Voice_production "Voice production") by a [human being](/wiki/Human_being "Human being") using the [vocal tract](/wiki/Vocal_tract "Vocal tract"), including [talking](/wiki/Speech "Speech"), [singing](/wiki/Singing "Singing"), [laughing](/wiki/Laughter "Laughter"), [crying](/wiki/Crying "Crying"), [screaming](/wiki/Screaming "Screaming"), [shouting](/wiki/Shouting "Shouting"), [humming](/wiki/Humming "Humming") or [yelling](/wiki/Yelling "Yelling"). The human voice is specifically a part of human sound production in which the [vocal folds](/wiki/Vocal_folds "Vocal folds") (vocal cords) are the primary sound source. (Other sound production mechanisms produced from the same general area of the body involve the production of [unvoiced consonants](/wiki/Voicelessness "Voicelessness"), [clicks](/wiki/Click_consonant "Click consonant"), [whistling](/wiki/Whistling "Whistling") and [whispering](/wiki/Whispering "Whispering").)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Voice](/w/index.php?title=Voice&redirect=no "Voice"))

Sound made by a human being using the vocal tract

"Vocal" and "Voice" redirect here. For the Pet Shop Boys song, see [Vocal (song)](/wiki/Vocal_(song) "Vocal (song)"). For other uses, see [Human voice (disambiguation)](/wiki/Human_voice_(disambiguation) "Human voice (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Human_voice_spectrogram.jpg/330px-Human_voice_spectrogram.jpg)](/wiki/File:Human_voice_spectrogram.jpg)

The [spectrogram](/wiki/Spectrogram "Spectrogram") of the human voice reveals its rich [harmonic](/wiki/Harmonic "Harmonic") content.



The audio that is represented in the above spectrogram - a woman says "It's all [Greek to me](/wiki/Greek_to_me "Greek to me")" using her voice.

The **human voice** consists of [sound](/wiki/Sound "Sound") [made](/wiki/Voice_production "Voice production") by a [human being](/wiki/Human_being "Human being") using the [vocal tract](/wiki/Vocal_tract "Vocal tract"), including [talking](/wiki/Speech "Speech"), [singing](/wiki/Singing "Singing"), [laughing](/wiki/Laughter "Laughter"), [crying](/wiki/Crying "Crying"), [screaming](/wiki/Screaming "Screaming"), [shouting](/wiki/Shouting "Shouting"), [humming](/wiki/Humming "Humming") or [yelling](/wiki/Yelling "Yelling"). The human voice is specifically a part of human sound production in which the [vocal folds](/wiki/Vocal_folds "Vocal folds") (vocal cords) are the primary sound source. (Other sound production mechanisms produced from the same general area of the body involve the production of [unvoiced consonants](/wiki/Voicelessness "Voicelessness"), [clicks](/wiki/Click_consonant "Click consonant"), [whistling](/wiki/Whistling "Whistling") and [whispering](/wiki/Whispering "Whispering").)

Generally speaking, the mechanism for generating the human voice can be subdivided into three parts; the lungs, the vocal folds within the [larynx](/wiki/Larynx "Larynx") (voice box), and the articulators. The [lungs](/wiki/Lungs "Lungs"), the "pump" must produce adequate [airflow](/wiki/Airflow "Airflow") and air pressure to vibrate vocal folds. The vocal folds (vocal cords) then vibrate to use airflow from the lungs to create audible pulses that form the laryngeal sound source.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The muscles of the larynx adjust the length and tension of the vocal folds to 'fine-tune' [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)") and [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"). The articulators (the parts of the vocal tract above the larynx consisting of [tongue](/wiki/Tongue "Tongue"), [palate](/wiki/Soft_palate "Soft palate"), [cheek](/wiki/Cheek "Cheek"), [lips](/wiki/Lip "Lip"), etc.) [articulate](/wiki/Manner_of_articulation "Manner of articulation") and [filter](/wiki/Band-pass_filter "Band-pass filter") the sound emanating from the larynx and to some degree can interact with the laryngeal airflow to strengthen or weaken it as a sound source.

The vocal folds, in combination with the [articulators](/wiki/Speech_organ "Speech organ"), are capable of producing highly intricate arrays of sound.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The tone of voice may be modulated to suggest [emotions](/wiki/Emotion "Emotion") such as [anger](/wiki/Anger "Anger"), [surprise](/wiki/Surprise_(emotion) "Surprise (emotion)"), [fear](/wiki/Fear "Fear"), [happiness](/wiki/Happiness "Happiness") or [sadness](/wiki/Sadness#Vocal_patterns "Sadness"). The human voice is [used to express emotion](/wiki/Emotional_prosody "Emotional prosody"),[[5]](#cite_note-johar-book-5) and can also reveal the age and sex of the speaker.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) [Singers](/wiki/Singer "Singer") use the [human voice as an instrument](/wiki/Human_voice_as_an_instrument "Human voice as an instrument") for creating [music](/wiki/Music "Music").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Voice types and the folds (cords) themselves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_voice&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Voice types and the folds (cords) themselves")]

Main articles: [Vocal folds](/wiki/Vocal_folds "Vocal folds") and [Voice types](/wiki/Voice_types "Voice types")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Gray1204.png/500px-Gray1204.png)](/wiki/File:Gray1204.png)

A labeled anatomical diagram of the [vocal folds](/wiki/Vocal_folds "Vocal folds") or cords.

Adult men and women typically have different sizes of vocal fold; reflecting the male–female differences in larynx size. Adult male voices are usually lower-pitched and have larger folds. The male vocal folds (which would be measured vertically in the opposite diagram), are between 17 mm and 25 mm in length.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The female vocal folds are between 12.5 mm and 17.5 mm in length.

The folds are within the [larynx](/wiki/Larynx "Larynx"). They are attached at the back (side nearest the spinal cord) to the *arytenoids cartilages*, and at the front (side under the chin) to the [thyroid](/wiki/Thyroid "Thyroid") cartilage. They have no outer edge as they blend into the side of the [breathing tube](/wiki/Vertebrate_trachea "Vertebrate trachea") (the illustration is out of date and does not show this well) while their inner edges or "margins" are free to vibrate (the hole). They have a three layer construction of an [epithelium](/wiki/Epithelium "Epithelium"), vocal ligament, then muscle ([vocalis muscle](/wiki/Vocalis_muscle "Vocalis muscle")), which can shorten and bulge the folds. They are flat triangular bands and are pearly white in color. Above both sides of the vocal cord is the [vestibular fold](/wiki/Vestibular_fold "Vestibular fold") or *false vocal cord*, which has a small [sac](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sac "wikt:sac") between its two folds.

The difference in vocal fold size between men and women means that they have differently pitched voices. There is also [genetic](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics") variation amongst the same sex, with men's and women's [singing](/wiki/Singing "Singing") voices being categorized into types. For example, among men, there are [bass](/wiki/Bass_(vocal_range) "Bass (vocal range)"), [bass-baritone](/wiki/Bass-baritone "Bass-baritone"), [baritone](/wiki/Baritone "Baritone"), [baritenor](/wiki/Baritenor "Baritenor"), [tenor](/wiki/Tenor "Tenor") and [countertenor](/wiki/Countertenor "Countertenor") (ranging from [E2 to E5 and higher](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation")), and among women, [contralto](/wiki/Contralto "Contralto"), [alto](/wiki/Alto "Alto"), [mezzo-soprano](/wiki/Mezzo-soprano "Mezzo-soprano") and [soprano](/wiki/Soprano "Soprano") (ranging from F3 to C6 and higher). There are additional categories for [operatic voices](/wiki/Opera#Operatic_voices "Opera"), see [voice type](/wiki/Voice_type "Voice type"). This is not the only source of difference between male and female voice. Men, generally speaking, have a larger [vocal tract](/wiki/Vocal_tract "Vocal tract"), which essentially gives the resultant voice a lower-sounding [timbre](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre"). This is mostly independent of the vocal folds themselves.

## Voice modulation in spoken language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_voice&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Voice modulation in spoken language")]

Human spoken language makes use of the ability of almost all people in a given society to dynamically modulate certain parameters of the laryngeal voice source in a consistent manner. The most important communicative, or phonetic, parameters are the voice pitch (determined by the vibratory frequency of the vocal folds) and the degree of separation of the vocal folds, referred to as vocal fold adduction (coming together) or abduction (separating).[[11]](#cite_note-Rothenberg_BSD-11)

The ability to vary the ab/adduction of the vocal folds quickly has a strong genetic component, since vocal fold adduction has a life-preserving function in keeping food from passing into the lungs, in addition to the covering action of the epiglottis. Consequently, the muscles that control this action are among the fastest in the body.[[11]](#cite_note-Rothenberg_BSD-11) Children can learn to use this action consistently during speech at an early age, as they learn to speak the difference between utterances such as "apa" (having an abductory–adductory gesture for the p) as "aba" (having no abductory–adductory gesture).[[11]](#cite_note-Rothenberg_BSD-11) They can learn to do this well before the age of two by listening only to the voices of adults around them who have voices much different from their own, and even though the laryngeal movements causing these phonetic differentiations are deep in the throat and not visible to them.

If an abductory movement or adductory movement is strong enough, the vibrations of the vocal folds will stop (or not start). If the gesture is abductory and is part of a speech sound, the sound will be called [voiceless](/wiki/Voiceless "Voiceless"). However, voiceless speech sounds are sometimes better identified as containing an abductory gesture, even if the gesture was not strong enough to stop the vocal folds from vibrating. This anomalous feature of voiceless speech sounds is better understood if it is realized that it is the change in the spectral qualities of the voice as abduction proceeds that is the primary acoustic attribute that the listener attends to when identifying a voiceless speech sound, and not simply the presence or absence of voice (periodic energy).[[12]](#cite_note-Rothenberg_IF-12)

An adductory gesture is also identified by the change in voice spectral energy it produces. Thus, a speech sound having an adductory gesture may be referred to as a "glottal stop" even if the vocal fold vibrations do not entirely stop.[[12]](#cite_note-Rothenberg_IF-12)

Other aspects of the voice, such as variations in the regularity of vibration, are also used for communication, and are important for the trained voice user to master, but are more rarely used in the formal phonetic code of a spoken language.

## Physiology and vocal timbre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_voice&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Physiology and vocal timbre")]

The sound of each individual's voice is thought to be entirely unique[[13]](#cite_note-13) not only because of the actual shape and size of an individual's vocal cords but also due to the size and shape of the rest of that person's body, especially the vocal tract, and the manner in which the speech sounds are habitually formed and articulated. (It is this latter aspect of the sound of the voice that can be mimicked by skilled performers.) Humans have vocal folds that can loosen, tighten, or change their thickness, and over which breath can be transferred at varying pressures. The shape of chest and neck, the position of the tongue, and the tightness of otherwise unrelated muscles can be altered. Any one of these actions results in a change in pitch, volume, timbre, or tone of the sound produced. Sound also resonates within different parts of the body, and an individual's size and bone structure can affect somewhat the sound produced by an individual.

Singers can also learn to project sound in certain ways so that it resonates better within their vocal tract. This is known as [vocal resonation](/wiki/Vocal_resonation "Vocal resonation"). Another major influence on vocal sound and production is the function of the larynx, which people can manipulate in different ways to produce different sounds. These different kinds of laryngeal function are described as different kinds of [vocal registers](/wiki/Vocal_registers "Vocal registers").[[14]](#cite_note-Vennard-14) The primary method for singers to accomplish this is through the use of the [singer's formant](/wiki/Singer%27s_formant "Singer's formant"), which has been shown to be a resonance added to the normal resonances of the vocal tract above the frequency range of most instruments and so enables the singer's voice to carry better over musical accompaniment.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Vocal registration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_voice&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Vocal registration")]

**Vocal registration** refers to the system of vocal registers within the human voice. A register in the human voice is a particular series of tones, produced in the same vibratory pattern of the [vocal folds](/wiki/Vocal_folds "Vocal folds"), and possessing the same quality. Registers originate in *[laryngeal](/wiki/Larynx "Larynx")* functioning. They occur because the vocal folds are capable of producing several different vibratory patterns.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Each of these vibratory patterns appears within a particular *Vocal range* of [pitches](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)") and produces certain characteristic sounds.[[18]](#cite_note-Large-18) The occurrence of registers has also been attributed to effects of the acoustic interaction between the vocal fold oscillation and the vocal tract.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The term register can be somewhat confusing as it encompasses several aspects of the human voice. The term register can be used to refer to any of the following:[[20]](#cite_note-McKinney-20)

* A particular part of the [vocal range](/wiki/Vocal_range "Vocal range") such as the upper, middle, or lower registers.
* A [resonance](/wiki/Resonance "Resonance") area such as [chest voice](/wiki/Chest_voice "Chest voice") or [head voice](/wiki/Head_voice "Head voice").
* A [phonatory](/wiki/Phonation "Phonation") process.
* A certain vocal [timbre](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre").
* A region of the voice that is defined or delimited by vocal breaks.
* A subset of a [language](/wiki/Language "Language") used for a particular purpose or in a particular social setting.

In [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), a **register language** is a language that combines [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") and vowel [phonation](/wiki/Phonation "Phonation") into a single [phonological](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") system.

Within [speech pathology](/wiki/Speech_pathology "Speech pathology"), the term vocal register has three constituent elements: a certain vibratory pattern of the vocal folds, a certain series of pitches, and a certain type of sound. Speech pathologists identify four vocal registers based on the physiology of laryngeal function: the [vocal fry register](/wiki/Vocal_fry_register "Vocal fry register"), the [modal register](/wiki/Modal_register "Modal register"), the [falsetto register](/wiki/Falsetto_register "Falsetto register"), and the [whistle register](/wiki/Whistle_register "Whistle register"). This view is also adopted by many vocal pedagogists.[[20]](#cite_note-McKinney-20)

### Vocal resonation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_voice&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Vocal resonation")]

Main article: [Vocal resonation](/wiki/Vocal_resonation "Vocal resonation")

**Vocal resonation** is the process by which the basic product of phonation is enhanced in timbre and/or intensity by the air-filled cavities through which it passes on its way to the outside air. Various terms related to the resonation process include amplification, enrichment, enlargement, improvement, intensification, and prolongation; although in strictly scientific usage acoustic authorities would question most of them. The main point to be drawn from these terms by a singer or speaker is that the result of resonation is, or should be, to make a better sound.[[20]](#cite_note-McKinney-20)
There are seven areas that may be listed as possible vocal resonators. In sequence from the lowest within the body to the highest, these areas are the chest, the tracheal tree, the larynx itself, the pharynx, the oral cavity, the nasal cavity, and the sinuses.[[21]](#cite_note-Greene-21)

## Influences of the human voice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_voice&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Influences of the human voice")]

Main articles: [Voice projection](/wiki/Voice_projection "Voice projection") and [Evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution")

The [twelve-tone musical scale](/wiki/Chromatic_scale "Chromatic scale"), upon which a large portion of all music (western popular music in particular) is based, may have its roots in the sound of the human voice during the course of [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution"), according to a 2021 study published in [the Journal of Neuroscience](/wiki/The_Journal_of_Neuroscience "The Journal of Neuroscience"). Analysis of recorded speech samples found peaks in acoustic energy that mirrored the distances between notes in the twelve-tone scale.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Voice disorders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_voice&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Voice disorders")]

Main articles: [Vocal loading](/wiki/Vocal_loading "Vocal loading") and [Voice disorders](/wiki/Voice_disorders "Voice disorders")

There are many [disorders](/wiki/Voice_disorders "Voice disorders") that affect the human voice; these include [speech impediments](/wiki/Speech_impediment "Speech impediment"), and growths and [lesions](/wiki/Vocal_fold_nodule "Vocal fold nodule") on the [vocal folds](/wiki/Vocal_folds "Vocal folds"). Talking improperly for long periods of time causes [vocal loading](/wiki/Vocal_loading "Vocal loading"), which is stress inflicted on the [speech organs](/wiki/Speech_organs "Speech organs"). When vocal injury is done, often an [ENT](/wiki/Otorhinolaryngology#Laryngology "Otorhinolaryngology") specialist may be able to help, but the best treatment is the prevention of injuries through good vocal production.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Voice therapy is generally delivered by a [speech-language pathologist](/wiki/Speech-language_pathologist "Speech-language pathologist").

## Vocal cord nodules and polyps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_voice&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Vocal cord nodules and polyps")]

[Vocal nodules](/wiki/Vocal_nodules "Vocal nodules") are caused over time by repeated abuse of the vocal cords which results in soft, swollen spots on each vocal cord.[[24]](#cite_note-24) These spots develop into harder, callous-like growths called nodules. The longer the abuse occurs the larger and stiffer the nodules will become. Most polyps are larger than nodules and may be called by other names, such as polypoid degeneration or Reinke's edema. Polyps are caused by a single occurrence and may require surgical removal. Irritation after the removal may then lead to nodules if additional irritation persists. Speech-language therapy teaches the patient how to eliminate the irritations permanently through habit changes and vocal hygiene.
[Hoarseness](/wiki/Dysphonia "Dysphonia") or breathiness that lasts for more than two weeks is a common symptom of an underlying voice disorder such as nodes or polyps and should be investigated medically.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Wikiquote-logo.svg/40px-Wikiquote-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikiquote-logo.svg)

Wikiquote has quotations related to ***[Voice](https://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Voice "q:Voice")***.

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Wikibooks-logo-en-noslogan.svg/40px-Wikibooks-logo-en-noslogan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikibooks-logo-en-noslogan.svg)

Wikibooks has a book on the topic of: ***[singing](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/singing "wikibooks:singing")***

* [Free Voice analyzer and Biometrics displaying software from](https://web.archive.org/web/20060924104333/http://www.phon.ucl.ac.uk/resource/sfs/wasp.htm) [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London") (archived 24 September 2006)
* [The Head Voice and Other Problems](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/19493), 1917, by D. A. Clippinger, from [Project Gutenberg](/wiki/Project_Gutenberg "Project Gutenberg")
* [The Voice Foundation's official website](http://www.voicefoundation.org/)
* [The Anatomy of Singing](http://www.singwise.com/cgi-bin/main.pl?section=articles&doc=AnatomyOfVoice) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20110716073911/http://www.singwise.com/cgi-bin/main.pl?section=articles&doc=AnatomyOfVoice) 16 July 2011 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")
* [David Harper, vocal coach: A passion for the voice that never wanes](https://web.archive.org/web/20090911233946/http://www.opera-opera.com.au/archive/davidharper.htm) – Opera article (archived 11 September 2009)
* [Irish Voice festival official website](http://www.spiritofvoice.com/)
* [How the voice works – *The Voice Works Like a Car*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-zXGGRiQbCo) (video on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"))
* [Voice acoustics: an introduction](http://www.phys.unsw.edu.au/jw/voice.html) from the University of New South Wales.
* [Speak and Choke 1](http://www.abc.net.au/science/articles/2002/03/19/502634.htm), by Karl S. Kruszelnicki, ABC Science, News in Science, 2002.
